instruction: Judge whether the citation likely exists and whether the review claim is supported.

input.review_text: Twenge et al. reported a link between increased screen time and worse adolescent mental health.

citation.raw_text: Twenge J M, Joiner T E, Rogers M L, Martin G N. Increases in depressive symptoms, suicide-related outcomes, and suicide rates among U.S. adolescents after 2010 and links to increased new media screen time. Clinical Psychological Science. 2018. doi:10.1177/2167702617723376
citation.title: Increases in depressive symptoms, suicide-related outcomes, and suicide rates among U.S. adolescents after 2010 and links to increased new media screen time

retrieval_evidence.title: Increases in depressive symptoms, suicide-related outcomes, and suicide rates among U.S. adolescents after 2010 and links to increased new media screen time
retrieval_evidence.abstract: The study reports links between new media screen time and adverse mental health outcomes.

output.explanation: Metadata aligns strongly and the claim stays close to the evidence.
output.revision_suggestion: Keep the statement cautious and cite the population boundary.

instruction: Judge whether the citation likely exists and whether the review claim is supported.

input.review_text: Twenge et al. proved that screen time directly caused adolescent depression.

retrieval_evidence.title: Increases in depressive symptoms...

output.explanation: The paper suggests association rather than strict causality.
output.revision_suggestion: Rewrite as an association instead of a direct causal claim.

input.review_text: The cited work partially supports the claim that platform use is associated with stress, but not every subgroup effect.

citation.raw_text: Lee S. Platform use and stress among first-year college students. Higher Education Behavior. 2022.
citation.title: Platform use and stress among first-year college students

retrieval_evidence.title: Platform use and stress among first-year college students
retrieval_evidence.abstract: The study finds associations between heavier platform use and self-reported stress in first-year students.

output.explanation: The evidence supports the main association, but not every implied subgroup extension.
output.revision_suggestion: Keep the supported association and remove unsupported subgroup generalizations.
